title_en: The Extent of Neighborhood in Islamic Law
title_id: Batas Lingkup Tetangga dalam Hukum Islam
content_en:
One of the most practically important questions in the Islamic law of neighborly relations is the definition of who counts as a 'neighbor' for the purposes of the rights and obligations elaborated in the Quran and the Sunnah. The Arabic word for neighbor — jar — is broader in its scope than the English word, and the scholarly tradition has developed specific discussions about how far the circle of neighborhood extends and how the intensity of obligations varies with distance.

The traditional scholarly definition of neighborhood extends to forty houses in every direction — a definition attributed to the Companions of the Prophet and transmitted through the early scholarly tradition. This expansive definition reflects the social realities of the close-knit residential communities of early Islamic Arabia, where forty houses might encompass the entire immediate social world of a family. In the contemporary context, this definition requires interpretation: does 'forty houses' mean forty physically adjacent dwellings, a forty-house radius in every direction, or the forty closest households in a given residential area?

Al-Wusabi notes that contemporary scholars have generally maintained the spirit of the forty-house definition while acknowledging the need for contextual interpretation in different residential environments. In a dense urban apartment building, one's neighbors include all occupants of the building and potentially residents of adjacent buildings. In a suburban neighborhood, the forty closest households on one's street and the surrounding streets constitute the relevant neighborhood. In rural settings, the circle of neighborhood might extend considerably further in physical terms to encompass the relevant social community.

The Quranic distinction between the near neighbor (al-jar al-junub in some readings) and the neighbor farther away establishes the principle that the intensity of the obligation is greater toward those who are physically closer. The family in the immediately adjacent dwelling — who share a wall, a garden boundary, or a common hallway — has the strongest claim on one's consideration and active care. The intensity of obligation diminishes gradually as the physical and social distance increases, but it does not disappear entirely until one has moved beyond the broad circle of neighborhood.

Islamic law also addresses the complex situations that arise when multiple neighborhood relationships overlap. A person who is both a relative and a neighbor has a compounded claim: the rights of kinship and the rights of neighborhood combine to create an even stronger obligation. A person who is a relative, a Muslim, and a neighbor has a triple claim. The Prophet specifically mentioned these combinations in the hadith tradition, establishing a graduated hierarchy of obligations that recognizes the practical reality that people occupy multiple social relationships simultaneously.

The question of the neighbor's right in the case of the sale of property has also been addressed by Islamic scholars. The principle of shuf'a — the neighbor's right of preemption — holds that in certain circumstances, the adjacent neighbor has the right to purchase property that is being sold, at the same price offered by any third-party buyer, before that third party can complete the purchase. This right, which differs in scope and application across the four Sunni schools, reflects the Islamic understanding that the neighbor's established interest in the property's use and character gives them a legitimate prior claim over a stranger. The Hanafi school extends this right most broadly, while the Shafi'i school limits it to co-owners rather than mere neighbors.
content_id:
Salah satu pertanyaan yang paling penting secara praktis dalam hukum Islam tentang hubungan bertetangga adalah definisi tentang siapa yang dianggap sebagai 'tetangga' untuk keperluan hak-hak dan kewajiban-kewajiban yang dijabarkan dalam Al-Quran dan Sunnah. Kata Arab untuk tetangga — jar — lebih luas cakupannya daripada kata Inggris, dan tradisi ilmiah telah mengembangkan diskusi-diskusi khusus tentang seberapa jauh lingkaran bertetangga meluas dan bagaimana intensitas kewajiban bervariasi sesuai jarak.

Definisi ilmiah tradisional tentang bertetangga meluas hingga empat puluh rumah ke setiap arah — definisi yang dinisbahkan kepada para Sahabat Nabi ﷺ dan ditransmisikan melalui tradisi ilmiah awal. Definisi yang luas ini mencerminkan realitas sosial komunitas perumahan yang erat di Arabia Islam awal, di mana empat puluh rumah mungkin mencakup seluruh dunia sosial langsung dari sebuah keluarga. Dalam konteks kontemporer, definisi ini memerlukan penafsiran: apakah 'empat puluh rumah' berarti empat puluh tempat tinggal yang berdekatan secara fisik, radius empat puluh rumah ke setiap arah, atau empat puluh rumah tangga terdekat di area perumahan tertentu?

Al-Wusabi mencatat bahwa ulama kontemporer umumnya telah mempertahankan semangat definisi empat puluh rumah sambil mengakui perlunya penafsiran kontekstual dalam lingkungan perumahan yang berbeda. Di gedung apartemen perkotaan yang padat, tetangga seseorang mencakup semua penghuni gedung dan berpotensi mencakup penghuni gedung-gedung yang berdekatan. Di lingkungan perumahan pinggiran kota, empat puluh rumah tangga terdekat di jalan seseorang dan jalan-jalan di sekitarnya merupakan lingkungan bertetangga yang relevan. Di lingkungan pedesaan, lingkaran bertetangga mungkin meluas jauh lebih jauh secara fisik untuk mencakup komunitas sosial yang relevan.

Pembedaan Al-Quran antara tetangga dekat (al-jar al-junub dalam beberapa pembacaan) dan tetangga yang lebih jauh menetapkan prinsip bahwa intensitas kewajiban lebih besar terhadap mereka yang secara fisik lebih dekat. Keluarga di tempat tinggal yang langsung bersebelahan — yang berbagi dinding, batas taman, atau lorong bersama — memiliki klaim terkuat atas pertimbangan dan perhatian aktif seseorang. Intensitas kewajiban berkurang secara bertahap seiring bertambahnya jarak fisik dan sosial, tetapi tidak hilang sepenuhnya sampai seseorang telah melampaui lingkaran bertetangga yang luas.

Hukum Islam juga membahas situasi-situasi rumit yang muncul ketika beberapa hubungan bertetangga tumpang tindih. Seseorang yang sekaligus merupakan kerabat dan tetangga memiliki klaim yang berlipat: hak-hak kekerabatan dan hak-hak bertetangga bergabung untuk menciptakan kewajiban yang bahkan lebih kuat. Seseorang yang merupakan kerabat, Muslim, dan tetangga memiliki klaim tiga kali lipat. Nabi ﷺ secara khusus menyebutkan kombinasi-kombinasi ini dalam tradisi hadis, menetapkan hierarki kewajiban yang bertingkat yang mengakui realitas praktis bahwa orang-orang menempati beberapa hubungan sosial secara bersamaan.

Pertanyaan tentang hak tetangga dalam kasus penjualan properti juga telah dibahas oleh ulama-ulama Islam. Prinsip syuf'ah — hak tetangga untuk mendahulukan pembelian — menyatakan bahwa dalam keadaan tertentu, tetangga yang berdampingan berhak untuk membeli properti yang sedang dijual, dengan harga yang sama yang ditawarkan oleh pembeli pihak ketiga mana pun, sebelum pihak ketiga tersebut dapat menyelesaikan pembelian. Hak ini, yang berbeda dalam lingkup dan penerapannya di antara empat madzhab Sunni, mencerminkan pemahaman Islam bahwa kepentingan tetangga yang sudah mapan dalam penggunaan dan karakter properti memberi mereka klaim yang sah lebih dari orang asing. Madzhab Hanafi memperluas hak ini paling luas, sementara madzhab Syafi'i membatasinya pada pemilik bersama daripada sekadar tetangga.
content_ar: One of the most practically المهم questions in the Islamic law of neighborly relations is the definition of who counts as a 'الجار' for the purposes of the الحقوق and obligations elaborated in the القرآن الكريم and the السنة. The Arabic word for الجار — jar — is broader in its scope than the English word, and the العلمي التراث has developed specific discussions about how far the circle of neighborhood extends and how the intensity of obligations varies with distance. The التقليدي العلمي definition of neighborhood extends to forty houses in every direction — a definition attributed to the الصحابة of النبي ﷺ and transmitted through the early العلمي التراث. This expansive definition reflects the social realities of the close-knit residential communities of early Islamic Arabia, where forty houses might encompass the entire immediate social الدنيا of a الأسرة. In the contemporary context, this definition requires interpretation: does 'forty houses' mean forty physically adjacent dwellings, a forty-house radius in every direction, or the forty closest households in a given residential area? Al-Wusabi notes that contemporary العلماء have generally maintained the الروح of the forty-house definition بينما acknowledging the need for contextual interpretation in different residential environments. In a dense urban apartment building, one's neighbors include all occupants of the building and potentially residents of adjacent buildings. In a suburban neighborhood, the forty closest households on one's street and the surrounding streets constitute the relevant neighborhood. In rural settings, the circle of neighborhood might extend considerably further in physical terms to encompass the relevant social الجماعة. The القرآني distinction between the near الجار (al-jar al-junub in some readings) and the الجار farther away establishes the principle that the intensity of the الالتزام is greater toward those who are physically closer. The الأسرة in the immediately adjacent dwelling — who share a wall, a garden boundary, or a common hallway — has the strongest claim on one's consideration and active care. The intensity of الالتزام diminishes gradually as the physical and social distance increases, but it does not disappear entirely حتى one has moved beyond the broad circle of neighborhood. Islamic law أيضاً addresses the complex situations that arise عندما multiple neighborhood relationships overlap. A person who is both a relative and a الجار has a compounded claim: the الحقوق of kinship and the الحقوق of neighborhood combine to create an even stronger الالتزام. A person who is a relative, a مسلم, and a الجار has a triple claim. النبي ﷺ specifically mentioned these combinations in the الحديث التراث, establishing a graduated hierarchy of obligations that recognizes the practical reality that people occupy multiple social relationships simultaneously.
